units.name: Self-supervised learning recap and scalable contrastive variants
units.description: Reviews the previous lecture's self-supervised learning setup and quickly situates MoCo, DINO, and DINOv2 before starting generative models.
units.summary: The lecture opens by revisiting self-supervised learning as unlabeled pretraining: an encoder-decoder is trained on a pretext task without human labels, then the decoder can be discarded and the learned representation transferred to smaller labeled tasks [ts=41s] [ts=71s]. It recalls image-based pretext tasks such as rotation, rearrangement, and reconstruction, then reframes the later part of the recap around contrastive learning, where different augmentations of the same image are pulled together while augmentations from different images are pushed apart [ts=130s] [ts=190s]. The instructor then summarizes why SimCLR needs large batches, explains MoCo's workaround of keeping a queue of earlier keys plus a momentum encoder updated as an exponential moving average of the main encoder, and notes that DINO replaces the loss with a KL-based objective [ts=324s] [ts=466s] [ts=537s]. The recap ends by emphasizing DINOv2 as a practically important self-supervised feature extractor whose main advance was scaling training from ImageNet-sized data to roughly 142 million images, yielding strong reusable visual features [ts=568s] [ts=598s].

key_points.text: Self-supervised learning uses pretext tasks on unlabeled data, then transfers the encoder to downstream labeled tasks.
Contrastive learning pulls together augmentations from the same image and pushes apart augmentations from different images.
MoCo keeps a queue of previous keys and updates a momentum encoder as an exponential moving average of the main encoder weights.
DINO replaces the SimCLR-style loss with a KL-divergence-based objective.
DINOv2 scaled training to about 142 million images and is highlighted as a strong practical source of image features.

units.name: Supervised vs unsupervised learning and probabilistic modeling basics
units.description: Sets up the lecture by separating learning paradigms from probabilistic model families and introducing how normalization creates probability-mass competition.
units.summary: Before diving into specific generative models, the instructor notes that the big practical change over the past decade was scale—more compute, bigger datasets, and more stable training—rather than a wholesale rewrite of the underlying mathematics [ts=634s] [ts=701s]. He then distinguishes supervised learning, where data are paired as (x, y) and the goal is to learn a mapping from inputs to labels, from unsupervised learning, where only x is observed and the aim is to uncover structure such as clusters, low-dimensional manifolds, or density estimates [ts=745s] [ts=806s] [ts=865s]. The lecture next introduces the separate axis of discriminative versus generative modeling, emphasizing that probability densities are normalized and therefore induce competition for a fixed unit of probability mass [ts=952s] [ts=969s] [ts=1001s]. In that framing, discriminative models learn p(y|x) so labels compete for each fixed input and the model cannot reject unreasonable inputs, while generative models learn p(x), letting all possible images compete and enabling explicit low-probability rejection of out-of-scope inputs; conditional generative models instead learn p(x|y) and make images compete within each conditioning signal [ts=1052s] [ts=1105s] [ts=1138s] [ts=1239s].

key_points.text: Supervised learning uses paired data (x, y) to learn a mapping from inputs to targets.
Unsupervised learning uses only x and seeks hidden structure such as clustering, dimensionality reduction, or density estimation.
A normalized density assigns mass across possible outcomes, so different outcomes compete for a fixed unit of probability mass.
Discriminative models learn p(y|x), so labels compete for each image but different images do not compete with one another.
Generative models learn p(x), allowing the model to assign low probability to unreasonable inputs and therefore reject outliers.
Conditional generative models learn p(x|y), so all possible outputs compete within each conditioning signal y.

units.name: Why generative models and how the model family tree is organized
units.description: Motivates conditional generation through ambiguity and then classifies major generative-model families by density access and sampling behavior.
units.summary: The main practical motivation for generative modeling is ambiguity: when one input y can correspond to many valid outputs x, a model should represent a whole conditional distribution rather than a single deterministic answer [ts=1637s] [ts=1671s]. The lecture uses language modeling, text-to-image generation, and image-to-video prediction to make this concrete, stressing that the value of p(x|y) is that it captures many plausible poems, images, or futures for the same prompt or starting frame [ts=1694s] [ts=1737s] [ts=1757s]. It then introduces a taxonomy of generative models by asking whether the model gives explicit density values or only sampling procedures: explicit-density models compute p(x), implicit-density models can sample without exposing p(x), and the tree further splits into tractable exact-density models, approximate-density models, direct samplers, and iterative samplers [ts=1820s] [ts=1843s] [ts=1912s] [ts=1940s]. Within that taxonomy, autoregressive models are the main exact explicit method covered today, variational autoencoders are explicit but approximate, GANs are direct implicit models, and diffusion models are indirect iterative samplers deferred to the next lecture; the instructor also warns that subsequent notation will often omit the conditioning variable y for simplicity even when the intended model is conditional [ts=1918s] [ts=1931s] [ts=1951s] [ts=1983s] [ts=1996s].

key_points.text: Generative models are especially useful when there are many plausible outputs x for the same input y.
Language modeling, text-to-image, and image-to-video are examples where p(x|y) captures output ambiguity.
Explicit-density models let you compute p(x), while implicit-density models let you sample without directly reading out p(x).
Autoregressive models are explicit tractable-density models, while VAEs are explicit approximate-density models.
GANs are direct implicit models and diffusion models are iterative implicit models.

units.name: Maximum likelihood estimation and autoregressive factorization
units.description: Develops exact-density training via maximum likelihood and shows how autoregressive factorization turns sequence modeling into next-token prediction.
units.summary: To move from taxonomy to concrete models, the lecture introduces maximum likelihood estimation as the generic way to fit an explicit density model: write a neural network for p(x; W), then choose parameters that make the observed training samples most likely under the model [ts=2114s] [ts=2153s]. Assuming IID data lets the joint likelihood factor across samples, and taking logs converts products into sums, producing the familiar log-likelihood objective used to train neural networks [ts=2265s] [ts=2296s] [ts=2321s]. Autoregressive models add the key structural assumption that each sample x can be split into an ordered sequence of subparts x1...xT, after which the chain rule gives a product of conditional next-part distributions [ts=2348s] [ts=2386s] [ts=2416s]. This makes RNNs and masked transformers natural autoregressive architectures for language [ts=2444s] [ts=2480s], but applying the same idea to images by rasterizing subpixel values into a huge discrete sequence is computationally expensive, which explains both the historical difficulty of pixel-level autoregressive image models and the need for more efficient tokenizations [ts=2582s] [ts=2653s] [ts=2693s].

key_points.text: Maximum likelihood estimation chooses model parameters that maximize the likelihood of the observed training data.
Assuming IID data lets the joint dataset likelihood factor into a product over independent samples, and log-likelihood turns that product into a sum.
Autoregressive models factor p(x) using the chain rule over an ordered sequence of subparts x1, x2, ..., xT.
RNNs and masked transformers implement autoregressive next-step prediction naturally.
Text fits autoregressive modeling well because it is already a 1D discrete sequence.
Rasterizing images into long sequences of subpixel values makes autoregressive image modeling feasible in principle but very expensive.

units.name: Plain autoencoders and the motivation for VAEs
units.description: Builds intuition for VAEs by first showing why a bottlenecked autoencoder learns features but does not by itself solve generation.
units.summary: The instructor temporarily drops the 'variational' part and first reviews standard autoencoders as an unsupervised way to learn features z from data x without labels [ts=2904s] [ts=2916s]. The basic training recipe is to encode x into a latent vector z, decode z back to x, and optimize reconstruction; the point is not to learn the identity function itself but to force useful structure through a narrow bottleneck so that z is far smaller than the original image [ts=2963s] [ts=2992s] [ts=3022s]. After training, that bottleneck representation can be reused for downstream tasks in the same spirit as other self-supervised methods [ts=3059s] [ts=3064s]. However, generation remains unsolved: sampling a z that matches the learned latent distribution is no easier than sampling x directly, so a plain autoencoder does not yet give a principled generative model [ts=3077s] [ts=3096s] [ts=3130s]. The VAE idea is to impose a known probabilistic structure, typically Gaussian, on the latent space so that after training one can sample z from that known distribution and decode it into new data [ts=3134s] [ts=3153s] [ts=3171s].

key_points.text: A plain autoencoder learns features z from x by reconstructing the input without labels.
Reconstruction is only useful when the latent code z is bottlenecked so the model must compress structure from the data.
The autoencoder's latent code can be reused as a self-supervised representation for downstream tasks.
Plain autoencoders do not solve generation because sampling z that matches the data-induced latent distribution is still hard.
VAEs address this by forcing the latent codes to follow a known distribution such as a Gaussian.

units.name: VAE latent-variable model and Gaussian encoder/decoder parameterization
units.description: Introduces the latent-variable generative story behind VAEs and explains how the encoder and decoder output distributions rather than single vectors.
units.summary: The formal VAE setup assumes each training image x^(i) was generated from an unobserved latent vector z^(i), with the latent prior usually chosen to be a simple unit Gaussian so that sampling is easy after training [ts=3206s] [ts=3230s] [ts=3274s]. A direct maximum-likelihood treatment would write p(x) by marginalizing over z, but the necessary integral over the decoder's neural-network input is intractable in high dimensions [ts=3315s] [ts=3365s] [ts=3410s]. The core variational trick is therefore to introduce a second neural network q_phi(z|x) to approximate the intractable posterior over latent codes while the decoder p_theta(x|z) models data conditioned on z [ts=3475s] [ts=3526s] [ts=3535s]. To make those networks output distributions, the lecture constrains them to Gaussian families: the decoder predicts a mean image with fixed diagonal variance, and the encoder predicts both a mean and a diagonal covariance for z [ts=3564s] [ts=3599s] [ts=3610s]. With a fixed decoder variance, maximizing Gaussian log-likelihood reduces to minimizing L2 reconstruction error, which explains the familiar reconstruction term that appears later in the VAE objective [ts=3664s] [ts=3738s].

key_points.text: The VAE generative story assumes each observed x was produced from an unobserved latent vector z.
A simple prior such as a unit Gaussian over z makes latent sampling possible after training.
Directly marginalizing z to compute p(x) is intractable because the decoder neural network makes the integral over z impossible to compute exactly.
VAEs introduce an encoder network q_phi(z|x) to approximate the posterior while a decoder network p_theta(x|z) models data generation.
The encoder outputs a Gaussian mean and diagonal covariance for z, while the decoder outputs Gaussian parameters for x.
With fixed decoder variance, Gaussian log-likelihood becomes equivalent to L2 reconstruction.

units.name: ELBO derivation, reparameterization, and VAE latent-space behavior
units.description: Derives the VAE training objective, shows how it is optimized in practice, and interprets the resulting latent space.
units.summary: The lecture derives the VAE objective by starting from log p(x), rewriting it with Bayes' rule, introducing q(z|x), and decomposing the result into an expected reconstruction term plus two KL divergences [ts=3794s] [ts=3890s] [ts=3936s]. The first interpretable term says that samples drawn from the encoder's q(z|x) should reconstruct x through the decoder, and the second pushes the encoder's latent distribution toward the chosen prior p(z) [ts=3957s] [ts=4003s]. The last KL term, KL(q(z|x) || p(z|x)), is intractable because p(z|x) was the quantity we could not compute in the first place, so it is dropped using the fact that KL divergence is always nonnegative; this yields a lower bound on log p(x), the ELBO, which the VAE maximizes instead of exact likelihood [ts=4051s] [ts=4086s] [ts=4147s]. Training then consists of encoding x into a Gaussian over z, applying the KL-to-prior penalty, sampling z with the reparameterization trick so gradients can flow, and decoding back to a reconstruction term [ts=4164s] [ts=4193s] [ts=4208s]. The segment closes by explaining the tension between reconstruction and prior losses—one wants distinct near-deterministic codes, the other wants codes close to a unit Gaussian—and connects that tension to practical sampling and to the common observation that varying latent dimensions can smoothly morph generated digits in interpretable ways [ts=4226s] [ts=4282s] [ts=4310s].

key_points.text: The VAE objective decomposes into an expected reconstruction term and KL-divergence terms after rewriting log p(x) with Bayes' rule and q(z|x).
The reconstruction term encourages samples from q(z|x) to decode back to the original x.
The prior term measures how closely the encoder's latent distribution matches the chosen prior p(z).
Dropping the intractable KL(q(z|x) || p(z|x)) term because KL is nonnegative yields the ELBO lower bound.
The reparameterization trick allows sampling z from the encoder's Gaussian while still backpropagating through the sampling step.
In a trained VAE, the reconstruction and prior losses fight to balance faithful reconstruction with a sampleable latent space, often producing smooth latent interpolations.
